name: Prayer (Tefillah)
shortDescription: Prayer (Tefillah) in Judaism is a central practice involving communication with God. It serves as a means of expressing gratitude, seeking guidance, requesting needs, and reaffirming faith.
description: Prayer, known as Tefillah in Hebrew, holds a central place in Jewish religious life. It is far more than simply reciting words; it's a multifaceted act of communication with God, encompassing expressions of gratitude, requests for needs, confession of sins, and affirmations of faith. Traditional Jewish prayer involves prescribed texts and rituals, yet personal and spontaneous prayer is also deeply valued. The most common forms of Jewish prayer are the daily services – Shacharit (morning), Mincha (afternoon), and Maariv (evening). These services contain specific prayers, readings from the Torah, and psalms. The Amidah, also known as the Shemoneh Esrei (Eighteen Benedictions), is the centerpiece of each service and consists of a series of blessings praising God, requesting needs, and expressing gratitude. The Amidah is traditionally recited while standing and facing Jerusalem. Prayer is considered a vital component of maintaining a relationship with God and is believed to have the power to influence divine judgment and bring about positive change. The intention (kavanah) behind the prayer is paramount, as sincerity and focus are considered essential for effective communication. The Temple in Jerusalem, particularly the Golden Altar used for incense offerings (Ketoret) played a central role in ancient Jewish prayer practices. While the Temple no longer stands, the concept of directing prayers toward Jerusalem remains a significant aspect of Jewish tradition. Prayer is often associated with purification and spiritual cleansing. Preparation for prayer may involve ritual washing (Netilat Yadayim) to ensure a state of purity. The act of prayer itself is seen as a way to purify the heart and mind, fostering a closer connection with the divine. Related concepts include repentance (Teshuvah), good deeds (Mitzvot), and the study of Torah (Torah learning), all of which contribute to a holistic spiritual life.
